tossups.question: Hummel’s 3rd Piano Concerto opens with a solo for this instrument. The scherzo of Mahler’s 7th Symphony begins with a solo for this instrument, as it plays on each upbeat while the rest of the orchestra joins. This instrument plays a “four D’s, down to A” theme to open Bach’s Christmas Oratorio. This instrument over pizzicato strings introduces the motif of the scherzo of Sibelius’s 1st Symphony. This instrument plays a “dotted quarter, eighth, quarter” motif on F after it is played on D and A to open the second movement of (*) Beethoven’s 9th Symphony. Four solo D’s on this instrument open Beethoven’s violin concerto, while Grieg’s piano concerto begins with a crescendo on this instrument before the soloist entry. An opening solo for this instrument inspired the nickname of Haydn’s 103rd symphony. For 10 points, name this large, pitched orchestral drum.
tossups.question_sanitized: Hummel's 3rd Piano Concerto opens with a solo for this instrument. Four solo D's on this instrument open Beethoven's violin concerto, while Grieg's piano concerto begins with a crescendo on this instrument before the soloist entry. An opening solo for this instrument inspired the nickname of Haydn's 103rd symphony. For 10 points, name this large, pitched orchestral drum.

tossups.question: Following a solo trumpet "bugle call," this instrument crescendos for two measures before the rest of the orchestra rejoins in the coda to the second movement of the "Military"symphony. The solo for this instrument in the "Agnus Dei" of the Mass in Time of War lends the mass an alternate nickname. In the first movement of the "Surprise" symphony, Haydn provided three measures for this instrument to retune. The opening passage for this instrument nicknames the penultimate London symphony, while its use in producing the fortissimo chord in the "Surprise" symphony lends that work its German nickname. Before Haydn, underlining trumpet and other brass was the primary job of this percussion instrument tuned with a foot-pedal. For 10 points, the "roll" for which Haydn's symphony 103 is nicknamed is played on what large copper drums?
tossups.answer_sanitized: timpani [or kettledrums; or timps; accept Paukenmesse; accept Paukenschlag; prompt on drums]

tossups.question: In one mass, a quiet solo from one of these instruments introduces a C-minor section of an Agnus Dei that follows a foreboding Benedictus reflecting the title Time of War. After a short solo from one of these instruments, the lower strings play a slow 3/4-time rendition of the Dies Irae theme at the beginning of an E-flat-major symphony. The “Surprise” Symphony is the earliest major work in which these instruments are tuned mid-piece, and they appear in its German (*) nickname. The second-to-last of Haydn’s London symphonies is nicknamed for its opening solo from one of these instruments. In Classical-era symphonies, this instrument often was only used to play tonic and dominant notes at the ends of cadences. For 10 points, name this most-common percussion instrument of the Classical era, a set of low, pitched drums.
tossups.answer: timpani [accept kettledrums; accept Paukenmesse; accept Symphonie mit dem Paukenschlag; prompt on the “Drumroll” Symphony or drums until read]
tossups.answer_sanitized: timpani [accept kettledrums; accept Paukenmesse; accept Symphonie mit dem Paukenschlag; prompt on the "Drumroll" Symphony or drums until read]

tossups.question: Solos for this instrument bookend the Burleske in D minor by Richard Strauss. In the Resurrection Symphony, the section “Der grosse Appell” (“dair GRO-suh AHP-pell”) introduces a third musician on this instrument to play offstage for fourteen bars, and then leave. In the scherzo of the Resurrection Symphony, this instrument also plays the opening solo. In the scherzo (“sker-tso”) of Beethoven’s Symphony No. 9, two statements of the opening (*) rhythmic motif are answered by this instrument. In the Classical period, this instrument was tuned exclusively to the tonic and dominant pitches of the piece being performed. But everything changed when the Dresden pedal system came about in the late 1800s, enabling this instrument to be re-tuned mid-performance. Pounding beats by this instrument punctuate the fanfare that opens Strauss’s Also sprach Zarathustra (“AL-zo sh’PRAK zara-THOO-struh”). For 10 points, name this percussion instrument which traditionally comprises four large drums.

Pounding beats by this instrument punctuate the fanfare that opens Strauss's Also sprach Zarathustra ("AL-zo sh'PRAK zara-THOO-struh"). For 10 points, name this percussion instrument which traditionally comprises four large drums.

tossups.question: This instrument and the double basses maintain the continuous sixteenth-note rhythm at the beginning of the “Infernal Dance” from Stravinsky’s The Firebird. A solo for this instrument opens the last variation before the fugue in The Young Person’s Guide to the Orchestra. In the first volume of the Concerto Project, Philip Glass’s Cello Concerto is paired with his “Concerto-Fantasy” for two of these instruments. Solos for this instrument open Beethoven’s Violin Concerto and Grieg’s Piano Concerto. A sliding foot pedal is used to tune this instrument. It is the only percussion used in Beethoven’s first eight symphonies, including in the “Pastoral” symphony, where it represents thunder. For 10 points, named this set of deep, pitched drums that often play “rolls.”

packet.name: K. Chicago A + Washington A + NYU A

tossups.question: In Prokofiev’s Romeo and Juliet, 15 straight notes from this instrument signify the death of Tybalt. Beethoven’s Eighth Symphony requires that these instruments be tuned specifically to play octave Fs, and in the scherzo of Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony, this instrument has a brief one-measure solo as the rest of the orchestra is silent. In the early 1800s, Gerhard Cramer’s technical innovations allowed for the rapid tuning of this instrument, which was developed from the Arabic naker. This instrument has a solo at the beginning of Beethoven’s violin concerto, and Haydn’s 103rd Symphony earns its nickname from the fact that the introduction features a long “roll” for this instrument. For 10 points, name this percussion instrument also known as the kettledrum.

tossups.question: Elliott Carter wrote Eight Pieces for four of these instruments. In the first movement of Beethoven’s fourth symphony, the leadup to the recapitulation prominently features this instrument. This instrument plays a counter-rhythm to the strings at the beginning of the third movement of Sibelius’s Violin Concerto. This instrument plays the first notes of Sibelius’s seventh symphony and Bartók’s third piano concerto. (*) Nielsen’s fourth features a “duel” of these instruments. Like the harp, this instrument uses pedals for tuning. This instrument has a solo at the beginning of Beethoven’s Violin Concerto, Grieg’s Piano Concerto and Haydn’s 103rd symphony. This instrument plays loud triplets on C and G in response to the trumpet fanfare at the beginning of Also Sprach Zarathustra. For 10 points, name this set of three to five tuned drums.

tossups.question: At one point in this work, the timpanist is instructed to tune his G-drum up to A, although many modern timpanists simply add a third drum. A variation in the second movement of this work features the oboe playing the melody of repeated sixteenth notes. Its first movement has two sections: an Adagio followed by a “Vivace assai” section. Students are commonly taught a rhyme making fun of the composer of this piece that is sung to the tune of the opening bars of its second movement and makes reference to his nickname (*) “Papa.” That Andante second movement of this work begins in 2/4 with the violins playing C, C, E, E, G, G, E. It is the second of the London symphonies. For 10 points, name this Joseph Haydn work whose second movement also includes a fortissimo G major chord anecdotally intended to “make the ladies jump.”
tossups.answer: Surprise Symphony or Haydn’s Symphony No. 94 in G or Sinfonie mit dem Paukenschlag or Symphony with the kettledrum hit [accept “timpani” in place of “kettledrum” and any synonym of “hit,” but do not accept “drum” in place of “kettledrum”; “Haydn” not needed after read]
tossups.answer_sanitized: Surprise Symphony or Haydn's Symphony No. 94 in G or Sinfonie mit dem Paukenschlag or Symphony with the kettledrum hit [accept "timpani" in place of "kettledrum" and any synonym of "hit," but do not accept "drum" in place of "kettledrum"; "Haydn" not needed after read]

tossups.question: It's not a string instrument, but Beethoven's Ninth requires these instruments to play a double stop. Two of these instruments disrupt the opening theme of the finale of another work by playing F/B and D-flat/G tritones. That work requires them to change their pitch while playing, features them at opposite ends of the orchestra, and is Carl Nielsen's "Inextinguishable" Symphony. In the original version of Grieg's Piano Concerto, they add to a horn crescendo by playing a namesake "roll." In another solo, these instruments play two notes over and over to represent hunters in Peter and the Wolf. For 10 points, identify this percussion instrument also known as the kettledrum.
tossups.answer: timpani [accept singular timpano; accept kettledrums before it is read]
tossups.answer_sanitized: timpani [accept singular timpano; accept kettledrums before it is read]

bonuses.leadin: This composer included the movements “choleric,” “melancholic,” “phlegmatic,” and “sanguine” in a symphony he fittingly titled The Four Temperaments. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this Danish composer whose fourth symphony includes a musical duel between two sets of instruments spaced far from each other on stage, giving it the nickname “Inextinguishable.”

Nielsen’s “Inextinguishable” Symphony includes a battle between two sets of these low-pitched percussion instruments which consist of a copper body. These tunable instruments are also called kettledrums.

Nielsen substituted cor anglais for a piece for this ensemble that closes with a third-movement “Praeludium – Theme with Variations.” Early Romantic composer Anton Reicha wrote a pioneering 24 pieces for this ensemble.

bonuses.leadin: Percussion instruments are key to providing rhythm and adding complexity in an arrangement. For ten points each, answer the following questions about instruments in the percussion section.
parts: This percussion instrument, also known as a kettledrum, is made of a copper bowl with a membrane stretched over it. These drums are tuned and typically appear in orchestras in sets of four or five.
This member of the mallet family consists of wooden bars with resonators underneath. This instrument has a wider range than xylophones and uses octave tuning.

Under the Hornbostel-Sachs system, timpani and most other drums are classified as membranophones under the Hornbostel-Sachs system. This other percussion category contains, in addition to marimbas, the triangle and maracas.

Martinů’s jazz pieces resemble the later work of this German-born composer of the song “Mack the Knife” from The Threepenny Opera.

Martinů’s best-known orchestral piece is likely his Double Concerto for two string orchestras, piano, and this instrument. The finale of Carl Nielsen’s Inextinguishable Symphony features a “duel” between two players of these instruments.

bonuses.leadin: Answer the following about the 20th-century musical tradition of writing a concerto for orchestra, for 10 points each.
parts: Arguably the best-known Concerto for Orchestra was written by this Hungarian composer of Duke Bluebeard’s Castle and Music for Strings, Percussion and Celesta.

Witold Lutosławski’s (“VEE-told loo-toh-SWAFF-skee’s”) Concerto for Orchestra begins with loud repeating notes from one of these instruments. Two players of these instruments “duel” in the finale of Carl Nielsen’s “Inextinguishable” Symphony.

This composer’s 1991 Concerto for Orchestra was written in the middle of a series of six compositions by this composer dedicated to conductor Marin Alsop (“MAIR-in AL-sop”), her Fanfares for the Uncommon Woman.

bonuses.leadin: This symphony opens with a slow introduction in D minor, which then proceeds to its first movement’s monothematic exposition. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this symphony, which is sometimes nicknamed for the impresario Johann Peter Salomon. At its premiere, the composer of this symphony remarked with pleasure about his revenues of 4000 gulden.

Like the first movement of the London symphony, many of Haydn’s other works in this musical form have monothematic expositions, meaning that their first two themes are nearly identical. This Classical-era form’s other main sections are the development and recapitulation.

bonuses.leadin: This instrument plays under the elegiac clarinet solo that opens Sibelius’s Symphony No. 1, and later introduces the theme of the scherzo third movement. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this instrument which plays the opening notes of the second movement of Sibelius’s Symphony No. 2. The third movement of Sibelius’s violin concerto begins with it playing a counterrhythm against the strings.

Sibelius’s tone poem The Swan of Tuonela uses a small percussion section of just timpani and this large, low-pitched drum. In a modern drum kit, it is placed upright on the floor and struck with the right pedal.

In Sibelius’s Symphony No. 2, this Italian-language instruction is placed at the end of the third movement, indicating that it should proceed directly to the fourth movement without a pause in between.
answers_sanitized: bass drum [or kick drum; or grancassa]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: This instrument plays under the elegiac clarinet solo that opens Sibelius's Symphony No. 1, and later introduces the theme of the scherzo third movement. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this instrument which plays the opening notes of the second movement of Sibelius's Symphony No. 2. The third movement of Sibelius's violin concerto begins with it playing a counterrhythm against the strings.

Sibelius's tone poem The Swan of Tuonela uses a small percussion section of just timpani and this large, low-pitched drum. In a modern drum kit, it is placed upright on the floor and struck with the right pedal.

In Sibelius's Symphony No. 2, this Italian-language instruction is placed at the end of the third movement, indicating that it should proceed directly to the fourth movement without a pause in between.

bonuses.leadin: Beethoven’s violin concerto opens with a solo for this instrument, while Philip Glass wrote a 2000 “Concerto Fantasy” that involves two soloists playing this instrument. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this instrument played by virtuosos such as Saul Goodman. Johann Christian Fischer wrote an unusual symphony for eight obbligato ones, and several Leonard Bernstein pieces call for them to be played with maracas.

This Hungarian composer required the lowest and highest drums of the timpani to play an F and E-flat while the two middle drums were pedaled consistently in his Concerto for Orchestra. He also wrote Mikrokosmos.

Two sets of timpani placed on opposite sides of the orchestra play a duel in the final movement of this symphony by Carl Nielsen. Nielsen wrote that this symphony was supposed to depict the "elemental will to live."
answers: Nielsen’s Symphony No. 4 Op. 29, FS 76 [or the “Inextinguishable” Symphony; or Det Uudslukkelige]
answers_sanitized: Bela Bartok [or Bela Victor Janos Bartok]
Nielsen's Symphony No. 4 Op. 29, FS 76 [or the "Inextinguishable" Symphony; or Det Uudslukkelige]
